---
title: "1.4 Assignment and Input"
subtitle: "Part 2: Data Types, null, Increment, and Input"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit1-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Data Types in Assignments

### Compatible data type

Every variable must be assigned a value before it can be used in an expression.

That value must be from a <span class="term">compatible data type</span> (兼容数据类型).

A variable is <span class="term">initialized</span> the first time it is assigned a value.

## Expression Type

### One value with one type

During execution, an expression is evaluated to produce a single value.

The value of an expression has a type based on the types of the values and operators evaluated in the expression.

For example, an arithmetic expression that uses at least one `double` value will evaluate to a `double` value and must be saved in a `double` variable.

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: assignmentTypes`

Textbook prompt: The code below looks okay at first glance, but if you run it, you will see that there is an error of incompatible types.

Change the data type of one of the variables to fix the error.

## Code Window

### `activecode:: assignmentTypes`

```java
public class Asgn
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
          int x = 1;
          double y = 2.2;
          x = 2 * y;
          System.out.println(x);
    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: assignmentTypes`

Runestone expects this output from `main` after the type fix:

```text
4.4
```

The assignment target must be able to store a `double` result.

## Reference Types

### `null`

Reference types like `String` can be assigned a new object or `null` if there is no object.

The literal <span class="term">`null`</span> is a special value used to indicate that a reference is not associated with any object.

```java
// String variables can be assigned null
// or a new String object
String str = null;
str = "new object";
```

## Adding 1 to a Variable

### `score = score + 1`

If you use a variable to keep score, you would probably increment it whenever score should go up.

You can do this by setting the variable to the current value of the variable plus one:

```java
score = score + 1;
```

The formula would look strange in math class, but it makes sense in coding because it assigns a new value to the variable on the left.

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: incrementScore`

Textbook prompt: Try the code below to see how score is incremented by `1`.

Try substituting `2` instead of `1` to see what happens.

## Code Window

### `activecode:: incrementScore`

```java
public class Test1
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        int score = 0;
        System.out.println(score);
        score = score + 1;
        System.out.println(score);
    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: incrementScore`

Runestone expects this output from `main`:

```text
0
1
```

## Input with Variables

### Same algorithm, different input values

Variables are a powerful abstraction in programming because the same algorithm can be used with different input values saved in variables.

Input can come in a variety of forms, such as <span class="term">tactile</span>, <span class="term">audio</span>, <span class="term">visual</span>, or the most common form, <span class="term">text</span> that the user types in.

The `Scanner` class in Java is one way to obtain text input from the keyboard.

## Scanner Example

### `activecode:: inputName`

The code below using the `Scanner` class will say hello to anyone who types in their name and will have different results for different name values.

First, type in your name below the code and then click on run.

Try again with a friend's name.

## Code Window

### `activecode:: inputName`

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class Main
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        System.out.println("Please type in a name in the input box below.");
        Scanner scan = new Scanner(System.in);
        String name = scan.nextLine();
        System.out.println("Hello " + name);
        scan.close();
     }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: inputName`

Runestone marks this as a Scanner example.

Keyboard input is useful and fun, although you will not be tested in the AP CSA exam on using Java input from the keyboard.

External IDE, Replit, JuiceMind, W3Schools, and Console links are not required in this static deck.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- explain that variables must be initialized before use
- assign only values from compatible data types
- explain why an expression with a `double` value needs a compatible target variable
- define `null` as a reference literal meaning no associated object
- trace `score = score + 1` as an update using the previous value
- explain that `Scanner` can store typed text input in a variable, though keyboard input is not an AP exam target
:::

## End

### Continue to Part 3

[Next: Dog Years coding challenge →](1-4-part-3-dog-years-challenge.html)
